Eleutherococcus is a genus of 38 species of thorny shrubs and trees in the family Araliaceae, native to eastern Asia, from southeast Siberia and Japan to the Philippines and Vietnam. These plants are often referred to as Siberian ginseng and are used in traditional Chinese medicine, where they are known as "cì wǔ jiā" (刺五加). Eleutherococcus senticosus, a species within this genus, is a small, woody shrub that has been studied for its chemical constituents, including isofraxidin-7-glucoside, a coumarin classified as a glucoside of isofraxidin. 

Amaryllis is the only genus in the subtribe Amaryllidinae, a small genus of flowering bulbs with two species. The better known species, Amaryllis belladonna, is native to the Western Cape region of South Africa and is known as the "March lily" due to its flowering period. Amaryllis is also associated with the common name "lily," though it is distantly related to the true lilies. The genus Amaryllis is sometimes confused with Hippeastrum, but the common name "amaryllis" is mainly used for cultivars of the genus Hippeastrum, which are widely sold in the winter for indoor blooming.
